title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about volunteering abroad. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Road Less Volunteered
content.subtitle: An article about the realities of international volunteering.
content.text:
Every year, thousands of young people pack their bags and head overseas with the intention of 'making a difference'. The brochures are appealing: smiling children, lush landscapes, and the promise of personal transformation. But beneath the glossy surface lies a more complex reality that few volunteers are prepared for before they board their flights. [31] The gap between expectation and experience can be profound, and not always in the ways volunteers anticipate. [/31]

Take Maya, a twenty-two-year-old from Bristol who spent three months teaching English in rural Cambodia. She had imagined herself at the centre of a thriving classroom, inspiring children who lacked opportunities. What she found instead was a well-run school with trained teachers who resented the implication that a well-meaning foreigner with no teaching qualification could do their job better. [32] 'I felt useless for the first month,' she admits. 'Not because there was nothing to do, but because I kept trying to do things that didn't need doing by me.' [/32] It was only when she shifted her focus — helping with a library catalogue, translating materials, and listening to what local staff actually needed — that her presence began to feel valuable.

The criticism most often levelled at short-term volunteering is that it benefits the volunteer more than the community. Psychologists who study altruism note that the desire to help is rarely as selfless as we believe. [33] 'Volunteering abroad offers a compelling package,' says Dr Helena Marsh, who has researched the motivations of gap-year travellers. 'You get adventure, a sense of purpose, social status back home, and something impressive for your CV — all wrapped up in the language of generosity.' [/33] This does not make the impulse dishonest, but it does complicate the idea that the primary beneficiary is the local community.

Nevertheless, dismissing all international volunteering as self-serving misses the point. Programmes that are long-term, skills-based, and developed in genuine partnership with local organisations have demonstrated real, lasting benefits. [34] The critical factor is not where the volunteer comes from, but whether the role was created because the community identified a need or because an organisation needed to fill a programme. [/34] The distinction sounds subtle but makes an enormous practical difference to outcomes.

For those still considering volunteering abroad, experts offer clear guidance. Research the organisation rigorously. Ask difficult questions about how projects are designed and monitored. [35] Be honest with yourself about your skills and what you can genuinely contribute over a realistic timeframe. [/35] Two weeks building a school wall may feel meaningful but rarely leaves a structure that lasts, whereas two months training local health workers in data recording can produce tools that outlive the volunteer's presence by years.

Ultimately, the most transformative volunteers tend to be those who arrive with humility rather than heroism. They understand that the world's problems were not waiting for them specifically to arrive and solve them. [36] What they offer instead is sustained attention, a willingness to learn, and the patience to be genuinely useful — qualities far harder to photograph for a brochure, but infinitely more valuable on the ground. [/36]

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the writer suggests that volunteers are often
options: A) misled by the organisations that recruit them.
B) surprised by the differences between what they expect and what they find.
C) unprepared for the physical demands of working abroad.
D) disappointed by the landscapes they encounter.
questions.explanation: The text says 'The gap between expectation and experience can be profound', indicating volunteers are surprised by the difference between what they imagined and what they actually encounter.

questions.question: What does Maya's experience in Cambodia illustrate?
options: A) Local teachers are unwilling to accept help from outsiders.
B) Three months is too short a time to make any real contribution.
C) Volunteers are most useful when they respond to actual needs rather than assumed ones.
D) Teaching is the least effective way for volunteers to contribute.
questions.explanation: Maya became effective only when she 'shifted her focus' to 'what local staff actually needed', showing the value of responding to real rather than imagined needs.

questions.question: Dr Helena Marsh's comment about volunteering abroad implies that
options: A) volunteers are deliberately dishonest about their intentions.
B) adventure travel and volunteering should be kept separate.
C) volunteers gain personal benefits that they rarely acknowledge openly.
D) organisations exploit volunteers' desire for adventure.
questions.explanation: Marsh describes the 'compelling package' of personal rewards — adventure, status, CV enhancement — all 'wrapped up in the language of generosity', suggesting these benefits are present but not openly admitted.

questions.question: According to the writer, the key to effective volunteering programmes is
options: A) the length of time volunteers commit to a project.
B) whether the project addresses a need identified by the local community.
C) the nationality and cultural background of the volunteer.
D) the quality of the partnership between volunteer and host organisation.
questions.explanation: The text states that the critical factor is 'whether the role was created because the community identified a need', not the volunteer's origin or the partnership itself.

questions.question: The writer advises potential volunteers to
options: A) avoid programmes that last fewer than two months.
B) focus on programmes that involve construction work.
C) realistically assess what they are capable of contributing.
D) choose organisations that have been established for many years.
questions.explanation: The advice to 'be honest with yourself about your skills and what you can genuinely contribute' means volunteers should realistically assess their own capabilities.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer argues that the most valuable volunteers are those who
options: A) make their work visible through photographs and social media.
B) bring specialist professional skills to difficult situations.
C) approach the experience with modesty and a desire to learn.
D) remain in their host country for at least one year.
questions.explanation: The writer says the best volunteers arrive 'with humility rather than heroism' and offer 'sustained attention, a willingness to learn' — qualities that describe modesty and openness to learning.
